one_line_takeaway: REGUN reframes approximate machine unlearning as distillation toward a class-conditioned held-out reference distribution rather than loss-maximization, and shows on ResNet-18 (CIFAR-10/100) and Swin-T (Tiny-ImageNet) that this yields a more favorable forgetting-utility trade-off than standard approximate baselines, with the clearest gains on the harder Swin-T/Tiny-ImageNet, high-forget-fraction setting — this is a general ML/privacy-utility method paper, not an AI-security paper (its only security-adjacent element is reusing an existing membership-inference attack, RMIA, purely as an evaluation metric).
key_finding: On ResNet-18/CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, REGUN attains the smallest overall gap to the RETRAIN baseline in several forget-fraction settings (e.g., CIFAR-10 at 1% forget: GAPRFTP_Avg 1.21 for REGUN vs 3.88 for BASE and 1.64-3.94 for other baselines; CIFAR-100 at 1% forget: RMIAAUC 46.91 for REGUN, the lowest among all methods, vs RETRAIN's own RMIAAUC of 49.56, indicating REGUN pushes membership-inference risk close to or slightly below the retrain-from-scratch reference). The paper explicitly notes the CIFAR-10 benchmark is 'close to saturated,' making differences among methods less discriminative there. The more notable result is on Swin-T/Tiny-ImageNet: REGUN achieves the lowest GAPRFTP_Avg among most compared methods and is described as 'the only method in our comparison to reliably reduce RMIA scores to the target retrain-from-scratch level' at higher forget fractions — concretely, at 50% forget, REGUN's RMIAAUC is 47.88 versus RETRAIN's 50.30, while BASE sits at 79.74 and other baselines (SALUN 58.04, AMUN 59.37, NEGGRAD+ 56.66) remain substantially higher; REGUN's GAPRFTP_Avg of 1.37 at 50% forget is the lowest in that table. A trade-off sweep (Figure 1, varying w ∈[0.1,0.9]) shows REGUN maintains more stable TESTACC as the forgetting-strength weight increases on Swin-T/Tiny-ImageNet, whereas other methods lose utility as forgetting strength rises.
core_contribution: The paper proposes REGUN (Reference-Guided Unlearning), an approximate machine-unlearning framework for image classifiers. Instead of degrading model performance on the forget set via loss-maximization/gradient-ascent-style heuristics (as in NegGrad-style baselines), REGUN treats unlearning as distillation: at each unlearning step it samples a forget minibatch, builds a class-conditioned 'reference distribution' by drawing held-out samples (disjoint from train/forget/retain sets) matched to the forget batch's label histogram, passes them through the frozen initial model to get a mean-of-probabilities target q(Bf), and trains the model to KL-match its forget-set predictions to q(Bf) while a cross-entropy retain term anchors performance on the retain set. The two named contributions (paper's own words): (i) introducing REGUN as a structured held-out-reference-based distillation approach to unlearning, and (ii) empirically validating it across multiple architectures/datasets/forget fractions in image classification, showing improved forgetting-utility trade-offs versus standard approximate baselines.
threat_model: Not applicable — this is not a security/attack paper. The paper's only adversarial component is its use of an existing membership-inference attack (RMIA, from Zarifzadeh et al. 2024, cited/reused as an evaluation tool, not a paper contribution) to audit residual information leakage about the forget set after unlearning. There is no novel adversary, attack surface, or defense proposed; MIA here serves purely as a utility/privacy-gap evaluation metric for the unlearning method.
limitations: Stated by the authors themselves: (1) the reference model f_phi is set to the original (pre-unlearning) model f_theta0 rather than the ideal oracle retrain-from-scratch model f_theta_r, because f_theta_r is unavailable in the approximate-unlearning setting — f_theta0 'still retains influence from Df,' a known imperfection in the reference signal. (2) The CIFAR-10 benchmark is reported as near-saturated across methods, limiting its power to discriminate REGUN's advantage. (3) The paper explicitly flags that Transformer-based unlearning is 'comparatively underexplored' and that performance gaps to RETRAIN are notably larger for Swin-T than for ResNet-18, attributing this to attention/representation-space challenges the current heuristics (including REGUN) do not fully solve. (4) The Conclusion lists unaddressed future directions as open limitations: the method as evaluated only uses class-conditioned references (not instance-conditioned or feature-space nearest-neighbor references); it is evaluated only on discriminative image classification, not generative modeling or high-dimensional foundation models. (5) The work is published at an ICLR 2026 workshop (not a main-track venue), consistent with its scope being a focused empirical study on standard image-classification benchmarks rather than large-scale or production settings.

quantitative_results: ResNet-18/CIFAR-10, forget 1%: REGUN GAPRFTP_Avg = 1.21 ± 0.26 vs RETRAIN 0.00, BASE 3.88 ± 0.90
ResNet-18/CIFAR-10, forget 1%: REGUN RMIAAUC = 48.90 ± 0.51 vs RETRAIN 49.98 ± 1.26, BASE 60.11 ± 1.31
ResNet-18/CIFAR-100, forget 1%: REGUN RMIAAUC = 46.91 ± 0.98 vs RETRAIN 49.56 ± 1.56, BASE 76.09 ± 1.32
Swin-T/Tiny-ImageNet, forget 50%: REGUN RMIAAUC = 47.88 ± 0.19 vs RETRAIN 50.30 ± 0.19, BASE 79.74 ± 0.05
Swin-T/Tiny-ImageNet, forget 50%: REGUN GAPRFTP_Avg = 1.37 ± 0.09, lowest among all compared methods in that column (e.g., AMUN 5.34, SALUN 5.36, NEGGRAD+ 4.12)
Swin-T/Tiny-ImageNet, forget 10%: REGUN TESTACC = 52.72 ± 0.25 vs RETRAIN 59.27 ± 0.30, REGUN RMIAAUC = 49.86 ± 0.83 (near RETRAIN 50.30 ± 0.66)
ResNet-18/CIFAR-10, forget 1%: RETRAIN TESTACC = 94.34 ± 0.02; REGUN TESTACC = 90.93 ± 1.14 (utility cost noted)
